concepts.lesson: Force is defined as a push or a pull acting on an object due to interaction with another object. Forces can change the motion of an object by starting motion, stopping motion, changing its speed, or changing its direction. A force can also change the shape of an object when applied strongly enough. Forces are present in many everyday activities such as kicking a ball, pushing a door, or lifting a bag. In science, force is measured in the unit called newton (N). Understanding force is essential because it explains how objects move and interact in the physical world.
concepts.real_example: Pushing a shopping cart in a supermarket
key_points: Force is a push or pull
Changes motion or shape of objects

concepts.lesson: A force can produce several effects on objects. It can make a stationary object move, stop a moving object, increase or decrease its speed, or change its direction of motion. Force can also change the shape of objects such as stretching a rubber band or squeezing a sponge. These effects demonstrate that forces influence how objects behave and move in the environment. By observing these effects, scientists understand how physical systems operate and how energy and motion interact.
concepts.real_example: Kicking a football changes its speed and direction

concepts.lesson: Contact forces are forces that act only when two objects are physically touching each other. These forces occur in many daily activities such as pushing, pulling, lifting, or dragging objects. Muscular force and friction are common examples of contact forces. Since the objects must be in direct contact, the force stops acting when the contact ends. Contact forces help us perform everyday tasks and control the movement of objects around us.
concepts.real_example: A person pushing a table across the floor
key_points: Examples include muscular force and friction

concepts.lesson: Non-contact forces are forces that act between objects without direct physical contact. These forces operate through fields and can influence objects from a distance. Common examples include gravitational force, magnetic force, and electrostatic force. For instance, the Earth pulls objects towards itself due to gravity, magnets attract iron objects without touching them, and electrically charged objects can attract or repel each other. These forces demonstrate that interactions in nature are not always dependent on direct physical contact.
concepts.real_example: A magnet attracting iron nails without touching them
key_points: Examples include gravity, magnetism, and electrostatic force

concepts.lesson: Friction is the force that opposes the motion of an object moving or trying to move across a surface. It occurs because surfaces in contact have tiny irregularities that interact with each other. Friction always acts in the opposite direction of motion and can slow down or stop moving objects. While friction can sometimes make movement difficult, it is also necessary for many activities such as walking, writing, and driving vehicles. Without friction, objects would slide uncontrollably.
concepts.real_example: A ball rolling on the ground gradually stops due to friction

concepts.lesson: Gravitational force is the force with which the Earth attracts objects towards its center. This force acts on all objects with mass and causes them to fall when dropped. Gravity is responsible for keeping planets in orbit, holding the atmosphere around the Earth, and giving objects weight. Unlike contact forces, gravity acts at a distance and does not require physical contact between objects.
concepts.real_example: An apple falling from a tree to the ground
key_points: Acts on all objects with mass

concepts.lesson: Weight is the force with which the Earth pulls an object toward itself due to gravity. Since weight is a force, it is measured in newtons (N). Mass, on the other hand, is the amount of matter present in an object and is measured in kilograms or grams. Mass remains constant regardless of location, while weight can change depending on the gravitational force acting on the object. For example, objects weigh less on the Moon than on the Earth because the Moon has weaker gravity.
concepts.real_example: A person weighs less on the Moon than on Earth
key_points: Mass remains constant but weight can change

concepts.lesson: Buoyant force is the upward force exerted by a fluid such as water or air on an object placed in it. This force acts opposite to the direction of gravity. When the buoyant force is equal to or greater than the weight of an object, the object floats. If the weight is greater than the buoyant force, the object sinks. Buoyancy explains why ships float on water and why objects like stones sink while wooden blocks float.
concepts.real_example: A wooden block floating in water
key_points: Depends on density of object and fluid
